meta.title: A217 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes A217 S‑Class Convertible engine — M276, M278, M176, M157, M177, M279. Real UK costs, the 2017 facelift changes, and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes S‑Class A217 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned A217 engine typically costs £2,500–£15,000 supply only, depending on engine — the M276 V6 sits at £2,500–£4,800, while the M279 V12 ranges up to £15,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500 depending on the variant. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes A217 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M176 4.0‑litre V8 (S 560, 2017–2020) — improved over the M278 it replaced, with better reliability and efficiency. The M276 V6 (S 400 4MATIC) is also a strong performer, though less powerful than the V8 options. For AMG buyers, the M177 is a significant improvement over the M157.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes S‑Class A217?

acceptedAnswer.text: Turbo cooling pipe failure on the M278 and M176 V8s, turbocharger and cooling system issues on the M157 AMG V8 (2015–2017), cooling system wear on the M276 V6, and general V12 complexity and parts availability on the M279 AMG V12.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes S‑Class A217 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine and maintenance history. The M276 V6 and M176 V8 are the most reliable options. The M157 AMG V8 (pre‑2017) carries known risks — check history before buying. The M177 AMG V8 (post‑2017) is an improvement. The M279 V12 is reliable given its extreme complexity but demands meticulous maintenance and carries high repair costs.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes A217 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a high‑mileage M157 (AMG S 63, 2015–2017) without documented turbo and cooling system history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A neglected M279 (AMG S 65) with V12 complexity is the second. All are fundamentally reliable with proper care.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my A217 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Coolant loss or turbo issues (M278/M176), turbocharger noise or cooling system failure (M157/M177), coolant warning light or temperature fluctuations (M276), or V12 complexity issues (M279) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes S‑Class A217 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £35,000 A217, a £6,500 M176 recon is 19% of the car's value — safe. Against a £70,000 AMG S 65, a £15,000 M279 recon is 21% — viable if you're in V12 territory. Our 70% Mercedes threshold is rarely crossed on A217s with strong values, but running costs — especially on AMG variants — tell a different story. Service history is everything.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes A217 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M276 V6, M176 V8, and M177 V8 are capable of 150,000+ miles with proper servicing. The M278 V8 is similarly durable with cooling system maintenance. The M157's limiting factor is turbocharger and cooling system wear — addressed proactively, it can still reach high mileage. The M279 V12 is capable of high mileage but maintenance costs escalate and parts availability can be an issue.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the A217 and C217?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both are S‑Class models built on the W222 platform. The A217 is the 2‑door convertible (this page, 2015–2020), while the C217 is the 2‑door coupé (2014–2020). The convertible has additional structural reinforcements to maintain rigidity, but shares most engines with the coupé. Costs vary slightly due to convertible‑specific components — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes A217 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate, in the vehicle documentation, and on the engine block itself. The 8th character of the VIN often indicates the engine variant. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date (pre‑ or post‑2017 facelift) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes S‑Class A217 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first‑ever S‑Class convertible since 1972 — from the M276 V6 to the M278 and M177 V8s, the M157 AMG V8, and the M279 V12 flagship. Real UK costs, honest failure data, and the engine that defines open‑top luxury. Backed by 1,250+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes S‑Class A217 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the A217 Convertible specifically — including the pre‑ and post‑facelift engine changes that define this grand tourer's ownership story.

overview.intro: The A217 is the convertible version of the S‑Class Coupé, unveiled at the 2015 International Motor Show Germany and produced until August 2020. It is the first S‑Class convertible since 1972, featuring lightweight reinforcements to maintain driving dynamics similar to the coupé. Based on the W222 S‑Class platform, the A217 was offered with a range of V6, V8, and V12 engines including AMG variants, with a 2017 facelift introducing new 4.0‑litre V8s. The A217 has no direct successor, making it a one‑generation model.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: W222 (Mercedes‑Benz S‑Class platform) • Predecessor: None (first S‑Class convertible since 1972) • Successor: None (one‑generation model) • Sibling body styles in this generation: C217 (coupé)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The A217 S‑Class Convertible generated significant UK engine enquiry demand in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], concentrated on the M278/M176 V8 and AMG V8 variants as these grand tourers enter their second decade.

items.quote: “The 4.0‑litre biturbo V8 replaced the M278 with improved efficiency and responsiveness — 469 PS and 700 Nm deliver effortless grand‑tourer performance. It’s the sweet spot of the A217 range.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want V8 refinement without AMG running costs.

“The 5.5‑litre biturbo V8 has known turbocharger and cooling system issues — owners report needing to replace multiple components to keep it reliable. The M177 that replaced it is a better bet.” Who it’s for: Buyers who understand the risks and have full service history.

“The 6.0‑litre V12 biturbo is the ultimate A217 engine — but the most punishing to repair. With just 630 PS and 1,000 Nm, the sheer complexity and rarity of this engine make a full replacement a five‑figure event.” Who it’s for: Collectors with deep pockets who want the V12 experience.

“The 4.7‑litre biturbo V8 delivers 455 PS of effortless power with better fuel economy than the AMG variants. It’s the refined V8 choice without the AMG price premium.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want V8 power without the running costs of the M157/M177.

“Often overshadowed by the V8 models, the 3.0‑litre biturbo V6 delivers 367 PS with lower running costs and better fuel economy. It’s the most sensible A217 engine choice.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want A217 ownership without the V8 tax.

economicsRule.title: The A217 rule
economicsRule.text: A £6,500 M176 recon against a £35,000 A217 sits at 19% — safe. A £11,000 M177 recon against a £50,000 AMG S 63 is 22% — still safe. A £15,000 M279 recon against a £70,000 AMG S 65 is 21% — viable if you’re in V12 territory. Our 70% Mercedes threshold is rarely crossed on A217s with strong values, but running costs — especially on AMG variants — tell a different story. Service history is everything.

keyTakeaways.question: Which A217 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M276 (S 400 4MATIC) — lower running costs, proven V6.

keyTakeaways.question: Which A217 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M279 (AMG S 65) — V12 complexity, turbocharger, and parts availability.

keyTakeaways.question: Which A217 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: M176 (S 560) — modern V8 refinement without the AMG running costs.

problems.title: M278 / M176 Turbo Cooling Pipe Failure (S 500 / S 560)
problems.rootCause: The M278 and M176 V8 biturbos have a known weakness — the turbo cooling pipes degrade and can fail, leading to coolant loss and potential turbocharger damage. The M176 is an improvement but cooling system maintenance remains critical.
problems.recommendation: If you own an A217 V8, have the turbo cooling pipes inspected at every service — an £800–£2,000 preventative replacement is far cheaper than the £3,500–£6,500 repair that follows a failed pipe.

cta.label: Compare M176 engine prices for your A217 S 560 →

problems.title: M157 Turbocharger & Cooling System Issues (AMG S 63, 2015–2017)
problems.rootCause: The M157 5.5‑litre biturbo V8 has well‑documented turbocharger and cooling system issues. Owners report needing to replace multiple components to keep the engine reliable. The M177 that replaced it is an improvement.
problems.recommendation: If you own a pre‑facelift AMG S 63, treat turbo and cooling system inspections as annual maintenance — a £1,000–£3,000 repair is far cheaper than the £5,000–£10,000 replacement that follows a catastrophic failure.

cta.label: Compare M157 engine prices for your A217 AMG S 63 →

problems.title: M276 Cooling System Wear (S 400 4MATIC)
problems.rootCause: The M276 V6 biturbo generates significant heat, and the cooling system components — water pump, radiator, and hoses — degrade over time.
problems.recommendation: If your S 400 develops a coolant warning light or temperature fluctuations, get the cooling system inspected immediately — a £500–£1,500 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500–£4,800 replacement that follows an overheating event.

cta.label: Compare M276 engine prices for your A217 S 400 4MATIC →

problems.title: M279 V12 Complexity & Parts Availability (AMG S 65)
problems.rootCause: The M279 6.0‑litre V12 biturbo is a masterpiece of engineering — but with extreme complexity and limited parts availability. Turbocharger issues and general V12 maintenance are costly and require specialist expertise.
problems.recommendation: If you own an AMG S 65, find a V12 specialist and treat annual inspections as non‑negotiable — a £1,500–£4,000 service is far cheaper than the £8,000–£15,000 replacement that follows a neglected failure.

cta.label: Compare M279 engine prices for your A217 AMG S 65 →

replacementCosts.h2: A217 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Engine Page baseline figures — A217 Convertible shares engine‑bay access with its C217 Coupé sibling, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are A217 Convertible only. C217 Coupé shares most of these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page, since fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style.

liveFeed.issue: Coolant loss, turbo cooling pipe inspection
